source.title: Add offline Helm OCI seeding and precompiled BPF-CNI module bundles for air-gapped kernels
source.author: Mateo Alvarez
source.release_notes: Adds support for precompiled BPF CNI bundles and a new offline Helm OCI seeder; improves airgap installer device/kernel compatibility and adds signed bundle verification
source.description: Motivation: Customers running Redwood Private in fully air-gapped datacenters reported two recurring issues: 1) inconsistent CNI installs when kernels differ from our build matrix, and 2) manual, error-prone steps to seed private OCI/Helm registries. This PR introduces an end-to-end offline-friendly flow: packaged, precompiled BPF-CNI kernel module bundles for common enterprise kernels plus a new OCI-Helm seeder sidecar utility that can be run from the airgap bootstrap container to atomically seed registries and charts. Summary of changes: packaged kernel module artifacts per distro (ubuntu20/22, rhel8/9, rocky, almalinux) with vmlinuz/headers checksums; an installer hook that probes target nodes and selects the closest-compatible module bundle; an offline Helm OCI seeder that pulls from provided artifact tarballs and imports images/charts into the local registry with optional signature verification; terraform module updates to create a registry seed job and a registry credential secret; Helm chart tweaks to surface device-mapper and module-loader knobs; and tests and preflight checks added to the installer preflight. Design notes: To avoid kernel compilation in customer environments we ship prebuilt, signed .ko bundles plus a fingerprint manifest. The installer probes uname -r and glibc/kernel headers and performs a checksum-based compatibility match; if no exact match exists, it falls back to a closest ABI-compatible bundle and inserts a graceful warning in the console output and audit log. The OCI seeder uses an idempotent import layer: tarball->verify->import->promote so retries are safe. Security: all bundles and helm charts are signed with our airgap signing key and verified locally before install; signature failure aborts the seeding step and surfaces a clear remediation path. Migration and rollout: added a canary flag in the installer config (installer.canary.offline_module_loader=true) to enable gradual rollout on Dedicated/Private installs. For major customer migrations we provide a helper script that collects /lib/modules and system info to help produce a custom bundle. Testing: added unit tests for the module selector logic, integration test harness that runs the seeder against a local registry in CI (recorded in ci/seeder-e2e), and manual validation on Ubuntu 20.04 kernel variants. CI run notes: initial CI run failed linter on variable naming in terraform module; fixed in second commit and all checks passed. Backwards compatibility: the default remains the previous build-from-source flow unless the canary flag is set. Documentation: added docs/private/offline-seeding.md and a troubleshooting section for kernel mismatches. Rollout plan: merge to main, cut a 0.31.0-alpha private-installer image with seeder sidecar, enable canary for internal staging clusters, gather telemetry on module fallback events before broad rollout. Related: ENG-12458 tracks broader vendor bundle matrix expansion.
commits: a1b3c9: add packaged module bundles and fingerprint manifest
f7d22ab: implement module-probe and selection logic in installer hooks
c12d3e4: add OCI-Helm seeder utility and import worker
b9e8f11: terraform: registry seed job and secrets
e2f44b6: helm: expose module loader config and device-mapper knobs
d4c9aaf: tests: module selector unit tests and seeder e2e harness
9b7cdef: docs: offline seeding guide and troubleshooting
review_comments: Priya Kapoor: Please add a clear audit log entry when the installer falls back to a non-exact kernel bundle. We need this for support triage.

Mateo Alvarez: Added an audit log line and included it in the doc troubleshooting steps (see docs/private/offline-seeding.md). Also added a telemetry tag 'module_fallback' to the installer heartbeats.
Ethan Ross: Terraform change looks good but we should add an expiration TTL on the registry seed job to avoid orphaned jobs.
Mateo Alvarez: Added ttl_seconds_after_finished to the Job spec and moved credentials to a k8s secret with limited RBAC scope.
Lena Müller: One security question: where is the signing key stored for airgap customers? We need to document the rotation pattern.
Mateo Alvarez: Documented recommended customer flow: use their KMS to sign bundles or request a signed bundle from Redwood; rotation guidance added in docs and an 'unbind' helper to revoke a bundle.
ci_details: ci/check-lint: failed on first run due to terraform variable naming, fixed in f7d22ab, rerun passed
ci/integration-seeder: flaked on local registry startup once, retried and passed
ci/build-images: built seeder and bundle-signer images successfully
source.merge_commit: 9f1a3bd: squash merge of offline seeder and module bundles
source.notes_for_support: If customers see 'module_fallback' events, advise collecting /var/log/redwood/installer.log and output of uname -r and /lib/modules; recommend requesting a custom bundle via support with the collected manifest. If signature verification fails, check that the provided signing key matches the bundle fingerprint and that network time is synced for validity window checks.
source.changelog_entry: Private-installer: Add offline OCI-Helm seeder, precompiled BPF-CNI kernel bundles, installer probes for kernel compatibility, and signed bundle verification. (canary behind installer.canary.offline_module_loader)
source.notes: This PR focuses on air-gapped reliability; follow-up work (ENG-12458) will expand the kernel matrix and add an automated bundle builder pipeline.

Documentation: added docs/private/offline-seeding.md and a troubleshooting section for kernel mismatches. Rollout plan: merge to main, cut a 0.31.0-alpha private-installer image with seeder sidecar, enable canary for internal staging clusters, gather telemetry on module fallback events before broad rollout. Related: ENG-12458 tracks broader vendor bundle matrix expansion.

source.title: installer: tenant-zone CMK placement + installer-signed-audit-envelopes
source.author: Evan Zhou
source.release_notes: Private installer: ensure customer CMKs are provisioned in the tenant's configured zone at install time; add an installer-side signer to produce immutable audit envelopes for on-prem/private deployments. Includes preflight checks and a reversible migration path.
source.description: Background: customers running private/on-prem deployments requested stronger guarantees that customer-managed keys (CMKs) are provisioned and used only within their configured data zone and that audit events emitted by the installer are tamper-evident for compliance audits. This PR adds installer-side enforcement of CMK placement (zone mapping) and an optional installer audit-envelope signer that produces a signed, immutable wrapper for audit batches so customers can retain a verifiable trail outside the control plane. Scope: - Preflight and onboarding validation for tenant->zone CMK mapping (installer preflight fails if CMK references a different region/zone) - Terraform + Helm changes to surface a tenant_kms_map var that binds tenant identifiers to allowed KMS zones - Installer KMS affinity controller that ensures API calls for key usage are routed only to allowed endpoints and rejects cross-zone CMK refs - An installer audit-proxy that signs audit batches with a tenant-scoped signing key (optionally backed by the customer's KMS) and writes signed envelopes to the configured audit sink - Migration helper: attempts safe rebind when an existing CMK is in an unexpected zone and emits a migration plan for operators - Feature flag: infra.install.kms_zone_enforce (default ON for new installs; existing installs are opt-in until migration is applied) Checklist: - [x] Preflight failing behavior implemented and unit-tested - [x] Terraform var + docs added - [x] Helm templates and chart values updated - [x] Audit envelope signer implemented with backward-compatible toggle - [x] Migration tool and a dry-run mode - [x] End-to-end smoke that runs in the CI private-installer job - [x] Security review (SEC-88) completed Design notes and motivation: preventing accidental cross-zone CMK usage reduces blast radius for data residency policies and is required by some customers' contractual controls. The signed audit envelopes provide an append-only proof for regulatory auditors without requiring the control plane to be fully trusted; operators can export these signed envelopes to their long-term retention stores. Implementation details: - installer/bin/preflight-check: new checks validate kms mapping and fail fast with suggested remediation commands - installer/terraform/kms_binding.tf: adds tenant_kms_map variable and validation rules; plan shows a migration recommendation if mismatched - helm/templates/kms-binding.yaml: renders Kubernetes secrets/configmaps that bind installer components to the allowed KMS endpoints - installer/cmd/kms-affinity-controller: light-weight controller that enforces runtime usage of mapped endpoints; rejects attempts to use unbound CMKs (returns 403 to caller) - installer/cmd/audit-signer: signs JSON-lines audit batches using PKCS#7 envelopes; verification helper included in installer/bin/verify-audit-envelope - charts/private-installer/values.schema.json: schema bump; new top-level keys under kms and audit documented - tests/ci/kms-affinity-smoke.sh and tests/unit/test_audit_signer.py added - docs/installer/kms-region-pin.md: operator facing doc and migration steps Rollout plan: - Canary: enable feature for a single internal tenant group in staging for 48h - Migration: run migration-tool dry-run across existing installs, notify operators of required action, then apply opt-in flag to migrate - Broad rollout: enable enforcement for new installs only for two weeks, then begin outreach to existing customers with an auto-migration path and operator-run command. Backout: toggling infra.install.kms_zone_enforce=false disables enforcement immediately; audit-signer can be disabled independently. Security considerations: the installer stores only a reference to the tenant signing key by default; signing may be delegated to customer CMKs via the KMS plugin. Access to signing keys is guarded by RBAC and the installer will log all signing operations as part of the audit envelope. Testing: unit tests added for preflight, controller rejection cases, and signer verification. CI: initial run flagged a flaky test that was hardened; final CI passed. Docs: new operator guide and migration playbook. Related: ENG-4012 (installer security hardening), SEC-88 (audit-envelope design).
source.commits: a1f3d2c: add tenant_kms_map var + validation; b4c2e11: helm templates for kms-binding + schema bump; c5d9e22: kms-affinity-controller implementation; d2b7f44: audit-signer and verify helper; e8a1f00: migration-tool and dry-run; f3a7c9b: tests and docs
conversation: Priya Das (reviewer): Please add a feature flag and make migrations opt-in; do not break existing installs without operator consent.
Evan Zhou (author): Added infra.install.kms_zone_enforce feature flag; migration tool now defaults to dry-run. PTAL.
Mateo Alvarez (reviewer): The signer should support delegating to customer CMKs — can we add a KMS plugin hook?
Evan Zhou (author): Added an optional kms.signing_delegate config and documented expected permissions. Tests updated to include a mocked KMS delegate.
Ruth Kim (reviewer): CI flaky on signer test; looks like a timing race when rotating ephemeral keys. Can we increase retry window?
Evan Zhou (author): Hardened signer tests and increased retry/backoff; reran CI.
CI Bot: initial pipeline had a flaky test failure in unit/test_audit_signer.py; re-run passed.
Priya Das (approver): security review completed — SEC-88 signoff.
Mateo Alvarez (approver): code LGTM, approve.
Merge: squash and merge by Evan Zhou via web UI (merge_method: squash)

source.title: add-transactional-tenant-seed-controller-and-quiesce-hook-values
source.author: Nora Patel
source.release_notes: Adds a transactional tenant-seed controller values schema and optional quiesce-hook scaffolding to enable safe, resumable tenant bootstraps and deterministic graceful quiesce behaviour during upgrades. No breaking changes to existing installs when new values are omitted.
source.description: Context and motivation: Private installer customers have hit flaky tenant seed runs and race conditions during rolling upgrades when tenant bootstrap jobs are requeued or retried. This PR introduces a values-first approach that wires an opinionated, idempotent tenant-seed controller configuration and a pluggable quiesce-hook scaffold so operators can opt into safe quiesce semantics during upgrades. Goals: (1) Provide a values schema for transactional seed semantics (lease, idempotency, backoff profiles), (2) Add templated quiesce hook that can be enabled per-tenant or cluster-wide, (3) Surface init telemetry toggles so the console and observability stack can correlate seed progress across retries. Changes are behind feature gates and default to backward-compatible behavior. Checklist: - [x] values.schema.json for charts/redwood-private with new top-level keys: seed.transactional, seed.backoffProfiles, quiesce.enabled, quiesce.gracePeriod - [x] templates/init-seed-job.yaml: new controller-driven mode + legacy job compatibility flag - [x] templates/quiesce-hook.yaml: hook template with leader-elector and optional preflight image verification - [x] chart helpers: functions for merging tenant overlays and defaulting backoff profiles - [x] unit tests for values validation and helm template smoke tests - [x] documentation stub in docs/helm/values-tenant-seed.md Related work: ENG-1429 (tenant bootstrap reliability), SUP-7241 (customer incident). Implementation notes: the seed controller config uses a small JSON blob that is translated into annotations on the seed job (leases, opaque id) so existing runners that don't adopt the controller remain functional. The quiesce hook is a lightweight Job template that runs as a pre-upgrade hook when enabled; it performs an idempotent drain + wait-for-kv-ack step and writes a hook-state ConfigMap key to guard rollback/unwind flows. Migration guidance: omitted values will continue to use legacy behavior; enabling "seed.transactional.enabled" triggers the controller-mode which is additive and reversible. See release notes for operational considerations.
commits: 7a3e09f: introduce transactional seed values schema and defaults
df4b2c1: add init-seed controller annotation wiring and legacy compatibility flag
5c9a20e: quiesce hook template with leader-elector and preflight image check
b1f6e4a: chart helpers: overlay-merge and backoff profile utils
c3d2f90: add helm unit tests and docs stub
changed_files: charts/redwood-private/values.schema.json (new) - adds seed.transactional, seed.backoffProfiles, quiesce.* and initTelemetry.* keys
charts/redwood-private/values.yaml - default profiles and feature gates added
charts/redwood-private/templates/init-seed-job.yaml - new Job template that supports controller-mode via annotations and legacy job path
charts/redwood-private/templates/quiesce-hook.yaml - pre-upgrade hook Job with leader-elector and idempotent guard ConfigMap
tests/helm/unit/values_test.go - validation for schema and overlay merging
docs/helm/values-tenant-seed.md - operational notes and upgrade guidance (doc stub)
review_comments: Diego Ramirez: Could we make the default backoff profile less aggressive for small clusters? Suggest changing the default maxRetries from 6 to 4. - author replied: lowered default to 4 and added rationale in values.schema.json description.

Ava Chen: Need an explicit opt-out path for clusters running pre-1.0 runners that expect the legacy job name. - author replied: added compatibility flag seed.legacyJobName and preserved job name when set; documented behavior.

Samir Gupta: CI recommended adding an integration smoke to ensure quiesce hook doesn't block on non-responsive external KV. - author replied: added a timeout and fallback behaviour to the template and unit test that simulates ack timeout.
QA (Mira Li): Verified helm template rendering with default and enabled transactional modes; no crashes. Small nit on doc phrasing fixed by author.
ci_checks: ci/unit-tests: pass (helm unit suite + values validation)
ci/linters: pass with minor formatting warnings fixed
source.merge_outcome: merged via squash into main. Merge summary: squashed 5 commits into one. Release note: 'Add transactional tenant-seed values and optional quiesce hook to improve bootstrap reliability and upgrade safety. Defaults are backward compatible.'
source.notes_for_ops: To enable transactional seed on a cluster set charts.redwood-private.seed.transactional.enabled=true and configure seed.transactional.controllerImage if using the controller mode. If you rely on legacy job names, set seed.legacyJobName to preserve behavior. Quiesce defaults are disabled; enable quiesce.enabled=true for maintenance windows. Observability: enable initTelemetry.enabled=true to emit seed.progress and seed.attempt_count metrics.
source.risk_assessment: Low-medium. All changes are feature-flagged and default to legacy behavior. Risk arises when operators enable transactional seed without adjusting backoff profiles for very large tenant counts. Recommend canary enabling on a single tenant before cluster-wide rollout.

source.title: introduce-sealedsecret-snapshot-rotator-with-transit-rekey-and-helm-upgrade-hooks
source.author: Maya Chen
source.release_notes: Add sealedsecret snapshot/rotation automation with optional transit rekey; Helm upgrade hooks to ensure backups before upgrade; Terraform module updates for KMS binding and RBAC; no user-facing API changes.
source.description: Context and motivation: Private deployments need an auditable, rollback-safe way to rotate secrets and keep recoverable backups of SealedSecrets when performing upgrades or rekey operations. Existing sealedsecret workflows at customers relied on ad-hoc scripts that are error-prone during multi-cluster upgrades and HSM/KMS key rotations. This PR introduces a controller and operational tooling that: 1) periodically snapshots SealedSecrets into a versioned encrypted archive, 2) stores snapshots in a user-configurable backend (S3-compatible or internal object store) encrypted with a transit KMS (Vault/AWS KMS) envelope, and 3) supports staged rekey/promotion with a safe rollback path. The design emphasizes minimal blast radius: snapshots are immutable objects with metadata linking to the KMS key version that encrypted them. Highlights and checklist: - New controller: sealedsecret-snapshot-rotator (daemonset-compatible, leader-elected) - Helm values: .sealedSnapshot.enabled, .sealedSnapshot.retention, .sealedSnapshot.backend, .sealedSnapshot.kms.transitKeyPath, .sealedSnapshot.preUpgrade.hookEnabled - Terraform: add module redwood_sealedsnapshot_kms_binding to create IAM/policies or Vault roles and bind them to installer service account - Upgrade hooks: pre-upgrade backup and post-upgrade verification hooks added to the Helm chart; hooks are idempotent and safe to re-run - Rekey workflow: a two-phase re-encrypt step that creates new snapshots with the new key while leaving previous snapshots intact; an emergency rollback command is provided to re-decrypt with prior key when necessary - Auditability: controller emits events and metrics; snapshots include manifest with resource names, namespace, SealedSecret controller version, and KMS key ID - Tests: e2e upgrade test that verifies snapshot + restore across chart version bump; unit tests for rekey logic - Docs: README and Upgrade playbook explaining operational steps and recovery procedures Checklist: [x] Controller implementation and unit tests [x] Helm chart changes + migration hooks [x] Terraform module for KMS binding and example usage [x] e2e test for upgrade + restore [x] Docs and runbook added What changed (high level commits): - implement snapshot rotator controller and reconciliation loop - add encrypt/decrypt using Vault transit and AWS KMS envelope support - add Helm chart templates for Job hooks and CronJob/DaemonSet config - add Terraform module and examples for IAM/Vault role bindings - e2e tests and CI workflow updates - docs: upgrade playbook, operator config reference Backwards compatibility notes: Controller is opt-in via Helm values (.sealedSnapshot.enabled=false by default). Existing SealedSecrets remain unchanged unless operator is enabled. Rekey operations require explicit CLI/workflow initiation by cluster operators to avoid accidental mass re-encryption. Security considerations: snapshots are encrypted with customer-provided transit key; we added KMS key ID binding in snapshot metadata and require RBAC to restrict snapshot read operations. Rollback safety ensures previous encrypted snapshots are kept until explicitly pruned. Related work: builds on ENG-2903 (sealedsecret lifecycle improvements) and incident notes from SUP-1289.
commits: c1a9f3d: add sealedsecret snapshot rotator controller and reconciliation tests
b4d2f8a: implement transit and aws-kms envelope encryption helpers
f8e1b0c: helm chart: add sealedSnapshot values and pre/post upgrade hooks
e2b7c52: terraform: new redwood_sealedsnapshot_kms_binding module and examples
d3c4a11: e2e: upgrade + restore test and CI workflow
a9f7d22: docs: add upgrade playbook and operator config reference
7b2cbb0: add metrics and events for snapshot operations
diff_snippets:
+++ helm/redwood-private-installer/templates/preupgrade-backup-hook.yaml
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: sealedsnapshot-preupgrade-{{ .Release.Name }}
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      serviceAccountName: {{ .Values.serviceAccount.name }}
      containers:
      - name: backup
        image: {{ .Values.sealedSnapshot.image }}
        args: ["backup", "--destination={{ .Values.sealedSnapshot.backend }}"]
      restartPolicy: Never
review_comments: Diego Alvarez: Nice end-to-end. Two asks before merge: add a unit test for the rekey path that simulates a mid-flight re-encrypt failure, and make sure the cronjob uses leader-election so it won't run in multiple replicas.
Maya Chen (author): Added the rekey failure unit test and switched the CronJob to a leader-elector wrapper; pushed new commits c1a9f3d -> b4d2f8a. PTAL.
Priya Nair: Terraform module looks good; please add an example showing Vault role binding semantics for HA Vault clusters.
Maya Chen: Added example and a short note about Vault HA and root-token-less bootstrap in examples/terraform/sealedsnapshot/README.md.
Ethan Park: CI flake on e2e; reran workflow and it passed on the second run. Approving pending squash-merge.
Release Manager (automated): Changelog entry suggestion: 'sealedsnapshot: add automated snapshot and transit rekey support (opt-in)'.
source.merge_summary: Merged by squash via GitHub UI after addressing reviewer feedback. Squash commit message enumerates controller, helm, terraform, tests, and docs. No conflicts with main. Deployment: Helm chart bump + one-time migration job (pre-upgrade hook) required for clusters enabling the feature.
operational_notes: To enable: set helm value sealedSnapshot.enabled=true and configure sealedSnapshot.kms.transitKeyPath and backend settings.
Pre-upgrade hook runs as Job; operators can set .sealedSnapshot.preUpgrade.timeoutSeconds to control window.
Snapshot retention defaults to 30 days; adjustable via .sealedSnapshot.retention and prune script (scripts/prune_snapshots.sh).
Rekey procedure: run `rotator rekey start --new-key=<path>` from operator CLI; monitor metrics sealedsnapshot_reencrypt_in_progress and sealedsnapshot_reencrypt_failed.
Rollback: `rotator rekey rollback --to-key-version=<old>` will re-emit events and make older snapshots active for restore; snapshots are kept until pruned.
Terraform: see terraform/modules/sealedsnapshot_kms_binding/README for IAM/Vault role binding examples; apply before enabling Helm chart to grant write permissions.

Highlights and checklist: - New controller: sealedsecret-snapshot-rotator (daemonset-compatible, leader-elected) - Helm values: .sealedSnapshot.enabled, .sealedSnapshot.retention, .sealedSnapshot.backend, .sealedSnapshot.kms.transitKeyPath, .sealedSnapshot.preUpgrade.hookEnabled - Terraform: add module redwood_sealedsnapshot_kms_binding to create IAM/policies or Vault roles and bind them to installer service account - Upgrade hooks: pre-upgrade backup and post-upgrade verification hooks added to the Helm chart; hooks are idempotent and safe to re-run - Rekey workflow: a two-phase re-encrypt step that creates new snapshots with the new key while leaving previous snapshots intact; an emergency rollback command is provided to re-decrypt with prior key when necessary - Auditability: controller emits events and metrics; snapshots include manifest with resource names, namespace, SealedSecret controller version, and KMS key ID - Tests: e2e upgrade test that verifies snapshot + restore across chart version bump; unit tests for rekey logic - Docs: README and Upgrade playbook explaining operational steps and recovery procedures Checklist: [x] Controller implementation and unit tests [x] Helm chart changes + migration hooks [x] Terraform module for KMS binding and example usage [x] e2e test for upgrade + restore [x] Docs and runbook added What changed (high level commits): - implement snapshot rotator controller and reconciliation loop - add encrypt/decrypt using Vault transit and AWS KMS envelope support - add Helm chart templates for Job hooks and CronJob/DaemonSet config - add Terraform module and examples for IAM/Vault role bindings - e2e tests and CI workflow updates - docs: upgrade playbook, operator config reference Backwards compatibility notes: Controller is opt-in via Helm values (.sealedSnapshot.enabled=false by default). Existing SealedSecrets remain unchanged unless operator is enabled. Rekey operations require explicit CLI/workflow initiation by cluster operators to avoid accidental mass re-encryption. Security considerations: snapshots are encrypted with customer-provided transit key; we added KMS key ID binding in snapshot metadata and require RBAC to restrict snapshot read operations. Rollback safety ensures previous encrypted snapshots are kept until explicitly pruned. Related work: builds on ENG-2903 (sealedsecret lifecycle improvements) and incident notes from SUP-1289.

source.title: Add mixed-version cluster compatibility and stitched-restore plan for cross-version migrations
source.author: Ethan Park
source.release_notes: Introduces a compatibility shim and "stitched restore" workflow that enables safe restores and rollbacks on mixed-version clusters. Includes Terraform inputs, Helm values, and an operator-side migration planner. Backward compatible by default.
commits: feat: add migration-compat shim and version probe API
feat: implement stitched-restore executor and dry-run mode
chore: terraform inputs for mixed-version cluster detection
fix: helm chart hooks to sequence restore and migration jobs
test: add integration harness for cross-version restore scenarios
source.description:
Motivation: Customers running private deployments often end up with mixed-version clusters during rolling upgrades, partial rollouts, or emergency hotfixes. Existing restore tooling assumes homogeneous cluster versions which leads to failed restores or irreversible schema changes. This PR introduces a lightweight compatibility shim plus a new 'stitched restore' workflow that can perform deterministic, reversible restores across mixed-version clusters without requiring full cluster-wide upgrades beforehand.

What's included: 1) compatibility shim: operator-side probe and per-node capability matrix (version_probe.go) that detects which nodes accept forward/backward compatible migrations and which require guarded operations. 2) stitched-restore executor: an orchestrator (stitched_restore.go) that constructs a deterministic sequence of migration and replay steps, splitting large migration sets into reversible micro-ops, and annotating each step with pre/post-checks. 3) Helm changes: new migration-job and restore-job templates with hooks to sequence jobs and respect node capability labels; values.yaml exposes feature flags (enableStitchedRestore, migrationConcurrencyLimit). 4) Terraform changes: inputs to surface cluster version skew and to provision a temporary 'restore coordinator' role. 5) Docs and runbooks: operator runbook with dry-run commands, how to interpret reconciliation annotations, and rollback guidance (installer/docs/*). 6) Tests: unit tests and an integration harness that exercises restores between v1.14 -> v1.16 and v1.16 -> v1.14 scenarios.

Design notes: - Stitched restore operates in three phases: probe, plan, execute. Probe collects node capabilities and current schema versions. Plan produces an idempotent, reversible plan where each migration is accompanied by a compensating reverse operation and data-shipping instructions for large backfills. Execute runs steps with pre/post-checks and optional snapshot checkpoints between critical steps. - Backups are "stitched" by annotating each snapshot with the migration state it was captured at; the executor will apply migrations in the exact recorded order needed to bring the cluster to the target state or to a safe rollback point. - The compatibility shim is intentionally minimal: it does not change model behavior, only reports capabilities and exposes a small API consumed by the executor.

Backward compatibility and safety: - Default is off: enableStitchedRestore is opt-in in values.yaml. - All migrations are run in a dry-run validation mode by default when invoked through the console or CLI; an explicit --apply flag is required. - Snapshots are mandatory before any apply step; the restore mechanism will refuse to proceed without a valid, recent backup. - The plan contains checkpoints; the operator will create logical checkpoints in object storage and record them in the cluster's restore CR for visibility.

Rollback strategy: - Each migration is paired with a reversible companion. If an operation is non-reversible (e.g., data-destructive drop), the planner will split it into safe phases and perform additional copies/backfills so the logical rollback remains possible. - The PR adds a 'rollback-preview' mode that simulates rollback steps and reports the delta without applying them.

Testing and validation: - CI added an integration pipeline that runs stitched-restore scenarios using the test harness in installer/tests/integration. The CI job composes two clusters (v1.14 & v1.16), runs a restoration from a snapshot taken on v1.14 into a mixed cluster, verifies schema integrity and application-level smoke tests, then attempts an automated rollback. - Local dry-run scripts are included (scripts/restore/dry_run_validator.sh) for SREs and support engineers.

Rollout and operational notes: - Feature gated; rollout should proceed with a canary tenant and the console flag enabled for a single region. - Recommend enabling migrationConcurrencyLimit=2 for initial runs to reduce live workload impact. - Operator exposes restore CR conditions: PlanReady, InProgress, CheckpointCreated, Completed, Failed. - Audit logs record each plan step and the checkpoint artifact ID.

Security: - The restore coordinator requires a scoped service account; Terraform module adds inputs to wire an optional KMS key for snapshot encryption. - All operator-to-storage interactions use signed URLs and short-lived credentials.

Open items / follow-ups: - Improve planner heuristics for large-table online backfills (ENG-2890). - Add metrics for step-level latency and per-step tokenization/IO (follow-up PR planned). - Add e2e tests for on-prem air-gapped restore scenarios (blocked on infra harness).

References: Linear ENG-2765, ENG-2890. Related runbooks and manual steps are in installer/docs.
review_thread: Priya Narang (reviewer): Can you clarify how the shim differentiates between incompatible schema changes vs safe nullable additions? Request more unit tests for the probe logic.

Ethan Park (author): Added explicit capability flags (schemaAddNullable, schemaDropNonDestructive, indexCreateOnline) and expanded unit tests in version_probe_test.go. See commit feat: add probe capability flags.

Amelia Chen (reviewer): Helm hook ordering looks fragile in clusters with older Tiller behavior; added a note to the chart and a retry loop in migration-job.yaml. Please confirm rollback-preview doesn't mutate state.
Ethan Park (author): rollback-preview is pure simulation and reads from the backup metadata only; added tests to ensure no CR modifications during preview.
Lucas Duarte (reviewer): Integration run failed once due to objectstore eventual consistency; added checkpoint verification/retry and a CI retry stanza.
Ethan Park (author): CI pipeline updated and rerun; integration passes on second run.
ci_checks: integration-stitched-restore: pass (rerun after objectstore retry fix)
test_plan: Step 1: Run dry-run plan against v1.14 snapshot -> expect PlanReady
Step 2: Execute apply with --apply flag on canary tenant -> expect CheckpointCreated
Step 4: Trigger rollback-preview and verify no changes
Step 5: Perform actual rollback from checkpoint and validate restoration

source.title: hook-priority-scheduler-and-template-dependency-resolution
source.author: Asha Kapoor
source.description: Introduce a hook priority scheduler and deterministic template dependency resolution for the private installer Helm charts and hook jobs. Motivation: tenants with large numbers of tenant-scoped init hooks were occasionally triggering concurrent heavy migrations and image pulls that caused network/registry rate limits and prolonged upgrade windows. This PR adds (1) a scheduler that assigns hook execution windows and priority buckets to init hooks via values.yaml configuration, (2) deterministic template dependency graph resolution so hooks declared with 'requires' ordering are applied consistently across helm renders and upgrades, and (3) an optional init-rate-limiter sidecar controller integration that coordinates cross-tenant concurrency using a ConfigMap-backed lease. The change includes: - new values schema fields (hookGroups, priorityBuckets, templateDeps, rateLimiter) with validation in values.schema.json - Helm template helpers for rendering hook annotations, leader-elector labels and per-tenant retries - A CRD-compatible annotation convention so the private-installer can surface hook group statuses to the installer controller (note: does not introduce a new CRD in this PR; uses annotated ConfigMaps for compatibility) - Unit and integration tests for template rendering and scheduler policy - Migration notes to preserve previous installs and a small upgrade hook to mark existing non-annotated hook resources for controller adoption Backwards compatibility: defaults keep existing behavior (all hooks single group, FIFO within group). Operators that opt in get controlled ramping and deterministic ordering. See linked Linear tasks and in-PR checklist for rollout steps.
commit_summary: scheduler: add hook-priority-scheduler library and policy config
helm: extend _helpers.tpl with renderHookMeta and resolveTemplateDeps functions
values.schema.json: add hookGroups, priorityBuckets, templateDeps and rateLimiter entries with validation
tests: add templating unit tests and an integration smoke harness for init-rate-limiter interactions
source.review_comments: Mateo: Can you make priorityBuckets optional when hookGroups is not set? Asha: yes, default is constructed in helpers.tpl when absent. Sophie: I noticed a race between the annotate-existing-resources upgrade hook and controller adoption in the first run; added a conditional backoff and a two-phase annotate step. Jordan: please add a note to the upgrade playbook about rate-limiter ConfigMap RBAC requirements. Once tests green I'll approve. After two CI reruns and a small mock time fix in policy_test.go approvals were provided and the PR was merged via squash.
source.release_notes: Add hook priority scheduling and deterministic template dependency resolution for private-installer Helm charts; introduce optional init rate limiter integration to coordinate hook concurrency across tenants. Backwards compatible by default; operators must opt-in via values.yaml. See docs/helm/hook-groups.md for configuration examples and the upgrade playbook for migration steps.
source.test_plan: Unit: policy_test.go covers priority buckets and dependency resolution permutations. Helm template tests validate rendered annotations and labels. Integration: init-rate-limiter-smoke runs a mini-cluster smoke test that simulates parallel tenant upgrades and asserts max concurrent hook jobs. Manual: follow upgrade playbook in docs/helm/upgrade-playbook.md to roll out on staging and verify annotate-existing-resources hook completes before controller adoption.
source.notes: This PR intentionally avoids introducing a CRD to reduce upgrade friction. If future work requires richer state, we'll propose a CRD migration path. Operators with strict air-gapped installs should pin the rate-limiter image and enable the sidecar controller via values to avoid external dependencies. The change was demoed during the infra sync on 2026-02-12.

source.title: add encrypted ENI bootstrap flow and deterministic DNS mirror rollback hooks
source.author: Maya Patel
source.description: Context: Customers deploying Private control planes in strict environments asked for stronger network-level guarantees: (1) control-plane ENIs must be provisioned encrypted at boot and bound to predictable IP allocations so firewall rules and egress policies can be pre-provisioned, and (2) DNS mirroring used during control-plane upgrades must support a safe rollback path that avoids left-behind stub zones or split-horizon drift. This PR implements an encrypted ENI bootstrapping flow in the Terraform installer, adds a deterministic IP assignment mode for controlplane subnets, introduces a lightweight Helm chart hook to seed a DNS mirror with signed zone metadata, and wires an automated rollback hook that re-scopes stub zone records and removes ephemeral mirror records on failed upgrades. Scope: terraform/modules/network/controlplane/* - new encrypted_eni module and deterministic_ip submodule; helm/controlplane/templates/dns-mirror-hook.yaml - pre-upgrade job to snapshot signed-records and post-failure job to reconcile; installer/upgrade/rollback.sh - idempotent rollback logic and a dry-run verifier; docs/installer/networking.md - operational notes and migration steps; test/terraform/controlplane_integ.tf - added integration fixture for encrypted ENI with mock KMS. Design notes: ENI encryption leverages customer KMS keys passed through the private-installer values and uses an immutable tag pattern (redwood:eni:hash) to detect re-attachment vs replacement during upgrades. Deterministic IP mode exposes a small allocator that maps a CIDR chunk + index -> stable IP and records the mapping in a tfstate-backed lock file so re-applies are stable across operators. DNS mirror hook writes signed RRsets into a namespace-backed ConfigMap and uses TTL pinning to avoid cache stomping; the rollback job reverses TTL pinning and prunes records only when lock metadata indicates the mirror was created by this release. Backwards compatibility: existing installs remain unchanged unless operators enable encrypted_eni or deterministic_ip via the new installer variables. Testing: added unit tests for allocator, a Helm integration run in CI that exercises pre-upgrade snapshot and a staged rollback scenario. Risks & mitigations: key rotation - the module refuses to re-encrypt existing ENIs without an explicit force flag; network policy mismatch - included a preflight verifier that checks security group rules against expected controlplane rule set. Checklist: - [x] Terraform module and unit tests - [x] Helm hook and rollback job - [x] Installer orchestration script - [x] Docs and migration guide - [x] CI integration run Summary of changes, commits and tests below.
commits: init: encrypted ENI terraform module and deterministic allocator
feat(helm): add dns-mirror preupgrade and rollback hooks
chore(installer): add rollback orchestration and dry-run verifier
test(terra): controlplane encrypted eni integ fixture
docs: networking.md migration notes and variables reference
fix: idempotency for tfstate lock creation
review_comments: Ethan Park: Left a few nits on variable naming and suggested extracting the KMS key validation to the preflight checks instead of the module. Please add a unit test for the allocator edge-case when the CIDR has an odd-sized range.
Maya Patel: Addressed the variable renames and moved KMS validation to scripts/preflight/checks.sh. Added allocator unit test and a guard for odd-sized CIDRs.
Leah Morgan: Approved after confirming Helm hook obeys helm.sh/hook-weight ordering and that rollback job tolerates API transient failures. Commented about adding metrics for rollback-run counts.

Carlos Ruiz: Noted a concern about state-lock expiry causing duplicate IP allocations during long operations. Recommended a lock TTL bump and detection to avoid overlapping allocations. Maya responded with a small retry backoff and a detection heuristic that refuses allocation if a recent allocation timestamp is present.
CI Bot: Initial pipeline failed on 'helm template' due to missing Chart.yaml dependency; fixed by adding Chart.yaml and re-running. Second run passed all integration gates.
Ops: Please add a migration note for customers with pre-existing ENIs — the module will not replace ENIs by default. Added to docs.
Leah Morgan: Post-merge request to follow up with a dashboard for rollback metrics (ENG-4821 follow-up).
source.release_notes: Introduce optional encrypted ENI bootstrapping for control plane, deterministic IP allocation mode, and safe DNS mirror/rollback hooks. Backwards compatible unless features enabled. Operators should read docs/installer/networking.md for migration steps.
source.notes_for_ops: Enable features by setting installer values: encrypted_eni.kms_key and deterministic_ip.enabled. Run installer preflight to verify SGs, KMS access, and CIDR suitability. Rolling back a failed upgrade should be invoked via installer/upgrade/rollback.sh --run, or CI will execute a dry-run on failures.
source.test_summary: Added allocator unit tests, terraform integration fixture, and a helm integration run. CI initially failed on helm lint due to a missing Chart.yaml dependency, rerun passed all checks. Post-merge nightly integ will run full e2e against a mock VPC.
source.operational_impact: Low for existing clusters; enabling deterministic_ip or encrypted_eni will introduce new resources and require KMS permissions. Rollback hook reduces risk of lingering DNS mirror records after failed upgrades.
source.followups: ENG-4821: add rollback metrics dashboard and alerting; ENG-4902: investigate providing automatic KMS key rotation helper; PM-121: add UI toggle to Console for deterministic IP mode.

title: add encrypted ENI bootstrap flow and deterministic DNS mirror rollback hooks
preview: Context: Customers deploying Private control planes in strict environments asked for stronger network-level guarantees: (1) control-plane ENIs must be provisioned encrypted at boot and bound to predictable IP allocations so firewall rules and egress policies can be pre-provisioned, and (2) DNS mirroring used during control-plane upgrades must support a safe rollback path that avoids left-behind stub zones or split-horizon drift. This PR implements an encrypted ENI bootstrapping flow in the Terraform installer, adds a deterministic IP assignment mode for controlplane subnets, introduces a lightweight Helm chart hook to seed a DNS mirror with signed zone metadata, and wires an automated rollback hook that re-scopes stub zone records and removes ephemeral mirror records on failed upgrades. Scope: terraform/modules/network/controlplane/* - new encrypted_eni module and deterministic_ip submodule; helm/controlplane/templates/dns-mirror-hook.yaml - pre-upgrade job to snapshot signed-records and post-failure job to reconcile; installer/upgrade/rollback.sh - idempotent rollback logic and a dry-run verifier; docs/installer/networking.md - operational notes and migration steps; test/terraform/controlplane_integ.tf - added integration fixture for encrypted ENI with mock KMS. Design notes: ENI encryption leverages customer KMS keys passed through the private-installer values and uses an immutable tag pattern (redwood:eni:hash) to detect re-attachment vs replacement during upgrades. Deterministic IP mode exposes a small allocator that maps a CIDR chunk + index -> stable IP and records the mapping in a tfstate-backed lock file so re-applies are stable across operators. DNS mirror hook writes signed RRsets into a namespace-backed ConfigMap and uses TTL pinning to avoid cache stomping; the rollback job reverses TTL pinning and prunes records only when lock metadata indicates the mirror was created by this release. Backwards compatibility: existing installs remain unchanged unless operators enable encrypted_eni or deterministic_ip via the new installer variables. Testing: added unit tests for allocator, a Helm integration run in CI that exercises pre-upgrade snapshot and a staged rollback scenario. Risks & mitigations: key rotation - the module refuses to re-encrypt existing ENIs without an explicit force flag; network policy mismatch - included a preflight verifier that checks security group rules against expected controlplane rule set. Checklist: - [x] Terraform module and unit tests - [x] Helm hook and rollback job - [x] Installer orchestration script - [x] Docs and migration guide - [x] CI integration run Summary of changes, commits and tests below.

source.title: Emit audit events during Private install/upgrade/rollback/config apply
source.author: Amina Yusuf
source.description: Context: Private customers reported gaps in audit coverage for operator-driven installer actions (notably upgrades and RBAC-related config applies) and Security needs parity with Hosted/Dedicated for “sensitive actions.” This PR instruments the Private installer to emit audit events for install/upgrade/rollback/config-apply/credential-rotation flows using the audit schema v1 envelope and event_type taxonomy. Summary of changes: (1) Add an audit emitter to the installer runtime that writes v1-shaped events to the local audit spool and forwards to the cluster audit ingestion endpoint when configured. (2) Emit events at the start + completion of sensitive workflows with consistent correlation (admin_action_id) and stable event_id for dedupe. (3) Capture operator identity where available (kubeconfig user / OIDC subject / OS user) without logging secrets. (4) Include deployment context: private_installation_id, cluster_id (if known), namespace, installer_version, and target version for upgrades. Event types added: private.install.start, private.install.complete, private.upgrade.start, private.upgrade.complete, private.rollback.start, private.rollback.complete, private.config.apply, private.credential.rotate. Field behavior: schema_version is set to 1.0; actor.type is “human” when we can resolve a non-empty operator identity and “system” when running unattended; actor.id is set to a stable string (OIDC sub preferred; else kubeconfig user; else local OS username) and actor.display_name is best-effort. source.ip is best-effort (only when invoked via the controller/remote runner, not local CLI). target.type is “private_installation”; target.id is the private_installation_id. changes.summary includes a capped diff summary (max 1KB) for config apply and upgrade plan; raw manifests are never logged. Delivery semantics: best-effort to remote audit sink; guaranteed local spool write unless disk is full. On sink failure, the installer continues and records audit_sink_status=degraded in the event outcome.reason. Tests: add unit tests for event shape validation + redaction, plus an integration test that runs an upgrade dry-run in CI and asserts emitted events match the required fields list. Notes: This is aligned with ENG-4826 and the schema enforcement work in ENG-4821/ENG-4830; exporter normalization (schema_version tagging) remains in audit-log-exporter PRs but this PR ensures producers already emit correctly shaped v1 events. Follow-ups: add operator identity support for air-gapped installs using explicit --operator-id flag (tracked separately), and wire installer audit spool rotation metrics into the Private observability pack.
commits: installer/audit: introduce AuditEmitter interface with v1 envelope builder
installer/audit: add local spool (newline-delimited JSON) with rotation + fsync
workflows: emit audit events for install/upgrade/rollback/config apply
identity: resolve operator from kubeconfig user/oidc/OS user with safe fallbacks
redaction: strip tokens, kubeconfig contents, and secret refs from changes.summary
tests: add audit event schema validation + golden fixtures
docs: add section on installer audit events and failure modes
source.illustrative_snippet: Example emitted event (redacted): {"schema_version":"1.0","event_id":"01HPYQ8Q4GJQ0V6B8WQ3YJ5F4G","event_type":"private.upgrade.complete","timestamp":"2026-02-13T22:41:09.114Z","tenant_id":"tnt_***","deployment_mode":"private","actor.type":"human","actor.id":"oidc:00u9abc...","actor.display_name":"sre@customer.com","target.type":"private_installation","target.id":"pri_2mX...","action":"upgrade","outcome":"success","admin_action_id":"admact_01HPYQ8J...","request_id":"","trace_id":"","changes.summary":"from=1.12.3 to=1.12.4; plan=rolling; components=control-plane,audit-exporter"}
review_conversation: Kevin Osei: Can we guarantee schema_version is always present even in spool-only mode? Also please add event_id generation rationale (ULID vs UUID) in docs.
Amina Yusuf: Yes—schema_version is set in the envelope builder before any sink interaction. Added docs note: ULID chosen for time-sort + SIEM friendliness; event_id stable per action attempt.
Hanae Suzuki: Redaction looks good, but please ensure we never log kubeconfig raw bytes or token prefixes in changes.summary. Add a regression test for the near-miss pattern ("Bearer ").
Amina Yusuf: Added denylist patterns for "Bearer ", "client-certificate-data", "client-key-data" and a unit test that fails if any appear in the serialized event.
Sean Gallagher: What happens if spool write fails (disk full)? We should be explicit: does the installer fail the workflow or proceed? Also add a metric/log line so support can diagnose.

Amina Yusuf: Proceeding with workflow (best-effort audit) but we now print a structured error line and set outcome.reason=audit_spool_write_failed when we can still emit to sink; if both fail, we log a single-line warning with action_id + failure cause.
Connor O'Brien: Please confirm these event_type names match the centralized registry. If the exporter expects different names, we’ll create churn for customers.
Amina Yusuf: Synced names with the taxonomy draft used in ENG-4826; Kevin confirmed they align with exporter normalization rules. Added a comment in docs pointing to the registry source of truth.
Vivek Kulkarni: For customer comms, can we avoid implying guaranteed remote delivery? Wording should be “best-effort remote + local spool when enabled.”
Amina Yusuf: Updated docs and PR description to match delivery semantics language used in the ADR draft.
source.release_notes: Private installer now emits v1 audit events for install/upgrade/rollback/config apply/credential rotation workflows, including operator identity where available. Events are spooled locally and forwarded to the configured audit ingestion endpoint on a best-effort basis; secrets are redacted by default.
source.security_notes: Do not log secrets: kubeconfig contents, bearer tokens, client key material, and raw manifests are explicitly excluded/redacted. changes.summary is truncated and denylisted to prevent high-risk leakage and high cardinality.
source.qa_notes: Validated against golden fixtures; ran upgrade integration test in CI; manually verified spool rotation on repeated config.apply actions.
related_links: Jira SUP-18421 (Private audit logs missing RBAC events)
Confluence: Audit Log Schema v1 + Sensitive Actions taxonomy (referenced in docs/audit-logging.md)

source.title: Add upgrade diagnostics bundle + failure hints for stuck migrations
source.author: Ethan Park
source.release_notes: Private installer: adds a support-friendly diagnostics bundle for upgrade failures (esp. stuck migrations) and improves error messaging with actionable hints and required artifacts list.
source.body:
Context: Support escalations for Private upgrades getting stuck at migrations are taking too long to triage because we’re missing a consistent artifact set and the installer’s failure output doesn’t guide the operator to the right next step. This PR adds a first-class diagnostics bundle and improves upgrade/migrations failure hints so Support/SRE/Platform can move from “stuck” -> “actionable” quickly. Ties to SUP-1845 and ENG-4828.

What’s included:
- New command: `redwood-private-installer diagnostics bundle --reason upgrade --output <path>`
  - Produces a single `.tar.gz` with a predictable directory structure.
  - Explicitly avoids collecting secrets (no K8s Secrets, no env dumps from pods). Basic redaction is applied to common token patterns in logs.
  - Includes a `MANIFEST.txt` with versions and timestamps to make email/Slack handoffs easier.
- Upgrade/migrations aware collection:
  - Installer logs for the attempted run (including last ~5k lines, plus full file if available).
  - Kubernetes resources relevant to the upgrade path (namespace-scoped):
    - `kubectl get pods,deploy,sts,job,cronjob -o wide`
    - `kubectl describe` for the migrations Job (if present) and any CrashLooping pods.
    - `kubectl logs` for migrations job pods (current + previous when supported).
    - Recent namespace events (last 2h) and warnings.
  - Helm status/history for the control plane release when deployed via Helm (non-fatal if not present).
  - Node pressure summary (disk/mem) from `kubectl describe node` (capped to the node(s) hosting control plane pods; best-effort).
- Clearer failure hints:
  - If the upgrade appears stuck at migrations (job running > threshold or repeated restarts), print a “What to check next” section with the exact commands to run and what output to attach.
  - Detect common classes of failures and emit targeted hints:
    - Image pull errors -> check registry reachability / pull secret configuration.
    - RBAC forbidden errors -> verify the installer service account cluster role binding.
    - Postgres connectivity / auth failures -> verify DB endpoint reachability and credentials rotation timing.
    - Schema lock / long-running migration -> provide guidance on collecting DB lock diagnostics (without exposing credentials) and when to escalate.
  - All hints include a “When to escalate” line that points Support to include the diagnostics bundle and reference SUP key(s).

Why this design:
- Support needs a single artifact they can request that doesn’t require live debugging on a customer cluster.
- The bundle format is stable so macros and KB articles can be copy/paste-ready.
- We intentionally do not attempt to auto-remediate. We only collect and guide to avoid surprising changes in regulated environments.

How to test:
- `make test` (unit tests for bundling/redaction + hint classification)
- Manual:
  - Run `diagnostics bundle` on a healthy cluster (should succeed and include expected files).
  - Simulate a failing migrations job (bad DB host) and confirm installer emits hint + bundle includes job logs/events.

Follow-ups (out of scope for this PR):
- Add a Support-facing doc page that mirrors the required artifacts + “stuck migrations” checklist (handled in ENG-4828 rollup).
- Add optional `--since` for events/logs window control (defaults are chosen to keep bundles reasonably sized).
commits: installer/diagnostics: add bundle scaffolding + manifest
installer/diagnostics: collect k8s events, pod status, job logs (migrations-aware)
installer/upgrade: classify common failures and print actionable hints
installer/diagnostics: redact common token patterns and cap log sizes
tests: add fixture-based tests for bundling and hint output
docs: update CLI help text + examples
files_changed_summary: pkg/diagnostics/bundle.go: collectors, manifest writer, size caps
pkg/diagnostics/collect_kube.go: `kubectl`-equivalent API calls via client-go, best-effort fallbacks
pkg/upgrade/upgrade.go: surface hints for stuck migrations and common failure classes
pkg/redact/redact.go: redaction rules (bearer tokens, common header keys) + tests
tests/diagnostics/bundle_test.go: validates deterministic bundle structure and manifest contents
review_conversation:
Sean Gallagher: Can we ensure we’re not collecting anything that could contain secrets (env vars, kube secrets, etc.)? Also, please add a one-liner that Support can paste into a ticket to request this bundle.
Ethan Park: Confirmed: no Secrets API calls, no env dumps. Added explicit allowlist collectors and a MANIFEST disclaimer. Also added a short macro-friendly snippet in CLI help output: `Please run: redwood-private-installer diagnostics bundle --reason upgrade --output ./redwood-diag.tar.gz`.

Naomi Feldman: Redaction looks helpful but regex-based redaction can be brittle. Can we keep the bundle internal-only unless customer explicitly approves sharing? Also cap file sizes.
Ethan Park: Added hard caps (default 5k lines per log stream + max 20MB per file) and a banner in the command output: “Bundle may contain sensitive operational details; share only via approved secure channel.” The installer already uses the Support portal upload flow; this aligns with that process.

Priya Natarajan: Great direction. Two asks: (1) include the installer version + git SHA in the manifest, (2) add detection for CrashLoopBackOff on the migrations job and print a hint.
Ethan Park: Done. Manifest now includes installer version + build SHA + timestamp. Added CrashLoopBackOff classification with guidance to include `kubectl describe pod` and previous logs.

Rafael Mendes: Suggest including a minimal node pressure snapshot. We see disk pressure causing opaque failures during upgrades.
Ethan Park: Added best-effort node describe for nodes hosting control plane pods, truncated to pressure + conditions sections to avoid huge output.
notes_for_support: If customer reports upgrade stuck at migrations, request diagnostics bundle and attach to SUP ticket: `redwood-private-installer diagnostics bundle --reason upgrade --output redwood-diag.tar.gz`.
Bundle is namespace-scoped and best-effort; if RBAC prevents collection, the tool will still produce a partial bundle and note missing collectors in MANIFEST.txt.
Escalation-ready artifacts: diagnostics bundle + approximate start time of upgrade attempt + installer version from MANIFEST.txt.

source.title: airgap: add apt/yum repo metadata + slim runtime bundles for offline installs
source.author: Ava Chen
commits: a1f4b2a: airgap: add generator for apt/yum repo metadata and Packages/repodata index
c9e7d11: packaging: produce slim runtime images (alpine-based) for init and probe containers
b3d0fae: installer: wire new bundle manifest format and repo-mapper in bundle extractor
e5f6c22: docs: airgap.md - offline repo setup instructions and GPG key rotation section
f9a2b88: tests: add integration test that validates apt repo served from local http and yum metadata correctness
source.description: Motivation: customers running on-prem/private installs often hit two pain points: (1) large monolithic tarballs that waste bandwidth and storage when only runtime artifacts are needed; (2) lack of standard apt/yum metadata in bundles makes tooling brittle for distributions that expect repo indices. This PR introduces a new bundle layout and tooling to reduce airgap bundle size, support OS package repositories out-of-the-box, and improve reproducible installs. Summary of changes: add airgap repo metadata generator that creates Packages.gz and repodata/repomd.xml for deb/rpm artifacts; add lightweight 'slim' runtime images for init, health-probe and migrator containers (alpine-based with static-linked helpers) and a build pipeline that outputs both full and slim images into the bundle; introduce an 'airgap-manifest.json' (minimal, stable schema v1) that lists package names, shasums, signed-gpg key IDs, and repository indexes for apt/yum + image index for containerd/CRI; update the bundle extractor to optionally populate a local apt/yum HTTP server (repo-proxy) used during install so machines can apt-get/yum install from the local repository without internet access; add helper script to import and rotate GPG keys used for repository verification; update helm chart packaging step to produce a helm-index.yaml inside the bundle and add a small init hook that can serve the chart archive to helm client during install; add integration tests that spawn a temporary HTTP server to validate apt/yum indices and verify package installation in a minimal Ubuntu 20.04 and Rocky Linux 8 chroot; docs updated with step-by-step airgap preparation, size expectations and migration notes. Checklist: - [x] generator for apt/yum metadata - [x] slim runtime images and packaging pipeline - [x] bundle manifest and extractor changes - [x] local repo-proxy implementation and tests - [x] GPG import/rotation helpers - [x] docs and upgrade notes Backwards compatibility: bundle extractor maintains support for the old flat tar layout; new manifest is optional and extractor will fall back if not present. Upgrade strategy: bundles produced by the new pipeline will be backward-compatible to older installers; however, to fully leverage slim images and repo indices, customers should upgrade the installer to >= the release that includes this PR. Related: hits SUP-3124 for on-prem support requests.
source.release_notes: Add optional airgap bundle manifest + apt/yum indices, introduce slim runtime images to reduce offline bundle size, and local repo-proxy to serve OS packages during install. Backward-compatible extractor fallback when manifest absent.
review_comments: Marcus Li: Requested that Packages.gz generation include debian-arch and component fields to play nicely with apt-mirror; also asked for deterministic ordering of files to improve reproducible shasums.
Ava Chen: Implemented deterministic sort and added component/arch metadata; updated tests to assert exact repodata hashes.
Diego Ramos: Asked for clear guidance on GPG key rotation and to surface errors during import rather than silently proceed. Left a suggestion for retry/backoff in networked imports.
Ava Chen: Added import checks, explicit exit codes and retry with exponential backoff in import_gpg_key.sh; documented behavior in airgap.md.
Priya Nair: Noted that slim images must pass security scan; requested that packaging pipeline push slim images to a staging registry in CI so scans can run. CI job was added and scans pass.
QA Team: Ran integration tests against Ubuntu20.04 and Rocky8 in ephemeral chroots; found one edge case for locales which was fixed by adding locale data to slim images.
source.merge_commit: squash-merge: add airgap repo metadata and slim images (PR 26111)
source.notes_for_ops: When generating bundles for customers: use --slim to produce both full and slim variants; include signed manifest with --gpg-key-id; recommended GPG key TTL is 2y and rotation process is documented in docs/airgap.md. Monitor repo-proxy logs (installer/extractor/repo_proxy.log) for 401/403 during package fetches in initial installs.
source.changelog: airgap: smaller offline bundles via slim runtime images; apt/yum repo indices included so offline installs can use native package managers; stable manifest v1 for bundle metadata; local repo-proxy for zero-internet installs.

source.title: Private installer: add safe optimization config rollback command (pin + verify)
source.author: Vivek Kulkarni
commits: installer/cli: scaffold `rwpi optimize-config` subcommand + wiring
rollback: implement `rollback` command w/ preflight + confirmation
state: persist last-known-good version in installer state file
k8s: add helper to read active config version from control plane endpoint
logging: emit structured logs and attach change_request_id
docs: add operator workflow and troubleshooting section
tests: unit tests for version parsing + API client retries; kind smoke test
source.description:
Context
Private deployments now support versioned Optimization Configs with safe apply + one-click rollback in Console. Private operators requested an equivalent workflow when Console access is unavailable (break-glass) or when rolling back during cluster/network issues.

This PR adds an operator-friendly rollback command to the `redwood-private-installer` CLI. The workflow is designed to be safe by default (explicit scope + confirmation), idempotent (retries won’t wedge state), and observable (structured logs + correlation IDs).

What’s in this PR
- New CLI command: `rwpi optimize-config rollback`
  - Rolls back the active Optimization Config version for a given scope by calling the Private control plane rollback API.
  - Supports explicit targeting:
    - `--tenant <tenant_slug>` (required)
    - `--model <model_id>` (optional; defaults to tenant-wide if omitted)
    - `--region <region>` (optional; defaults to installation region if omitted)
    - `--to-version <version_id>` (required)
  - Safety guardrails:
    - `--dry-run` prints the computed blast radius and the current/target versions without executing.
    - `--require-active-version` (default true) prevents rollback if the installer cannot confirm the currently active version.
    - Interactive confirmation with “type-to-confirm” using a deterministic confirmation string: `ROLLBACK <tenant>/<model-or-*>/<region-or-*> to <version>`.
    - Optional `--force` to bypass confirmation for automation (still logs a warning).
  - Emits a generated `change_request_id` (UUID) included in installer logs and passed through as an HTTP header for control plane/audit correlation.

- Preflight + verification
  - Preflight checks:
    - Installer can reach the control plane endpoint and is authenticated.
    - Target version exists and is compatible with the requested scope.
    - RBAC check: operator token must have the rollback permission.
  - Post-action verification:
    - Polls the control plane “active version” endpoint until it reports the target version or timeout.
    - Prints clear next steps when verification fails (includes curl commands and where to look in control plane logs).

- Installer state tracking (for safer operator workflows)
  - Records the last observed active version per scope in the installer state file to make it easier to revert during repeated incidents.
  - Does not attempt to be the source of truth; it is only used to improve operator UX (the control plane remains authoritative).

- Documentation
  - Adds `docs/operator/optimization-config-rollback.md`:
    - Example rollbacks
    - What “rolled back” means in Private (control plane pointer update + rollout controller applies to workers)
    - Common failure modes (RBAC denied, scope mismatch, verification timeout)

Example
- Dry run:
  `rwpi optimize-config rollback --tenant acme --model llama-3-70b --region us-east-1 --to-version ocv_01J0Z9... --dry-run`
- Execute:
  `rwpi optimize-config rollback --tenant acme --model llama-3-70b --region us-east-1 --to-version ocv_01J0Z9...`

Changed areas (high level)
- cmd/rwpi: command registration + flags
- internal/controlplane: API client additions:
  - GET active optimization config version (for verification)
  - POST rollback (with `change_request_id` propagation)
- internal/operator: confirmation + blast-radius formatting helpers
- docs: operator guide
- tests: unit tests + lightweight kind smoke test

Notes / design choices
- This PR intentionally does not implement fully automatic rollback loops. The command requires explicit target version and an operator confirmation by default.
- Verification polling defaults to 90s with exponential backoff; can be overridden via `--verify-timeout`.
- If the control plane rollback returns success but verification can’t confirm, the command exits non-zero to encourage explicit operator follow-up.

Rollout plan
- Internal use first (Redwood SRE + Private deployments on-call) during gamedays.
- Publish to Private operator docs after GA feature flag is enabled in Private control plane.

Follow-ups
- Add optional integration to annotate the customer’s Prometheus (if present) with rollback events.
- Support reading candidate rollback targets from the control plane version history to reduce manual copy/paste of version IDs.

Refs
- Linear: ENG-4829 (Private deployments sync + rollback), ENG-4825 (rollback endpoint)
- Related (monorepo): runtime rollback race fix in redwood/pr-20111
conversation:
Sean Gallagher (SRE): Can we make sure this fails safe if the installer can’t confirm the currently active version? During incidents we’ve seen partially degraded control plane endpoints and I don’t want operators rolling back blind.

Vivek Kulkarni (author): Added `--require-active-version` default true. If we can’t read active version, we abort unless `--force` is provided. Also `--dry-run` now prints a warning when the active version is unknown.

Marcus Lin (Security): Please confirm the rollback call is authorized via the same RBAC permission as Console rollback, and that we don’t log bearer tokens in debug output.

Vivek Kulkarni (author): Confirmed RBAC uses `optimization_config.rollback` on the control plane side; installer just surfaces 403 with a clear message. Also scrubbed Authorization headers from debug logs and added a unit test for the logger redaction helper.

Ethan Park (Control Plane): For verification, please hit the active-version endpoint rather than reading k8s objects directly. The rollout controller is authoritative and we need to keep the client contract stable.

Vivek Kulkarni (author): Updated implementation to use `GET /v1/optimization-config/active` (scoped), no direct k8s reads. Removed the earlier ConfigMap fallback.

Connor O'Brien (Release Eng): Any backward-compat concerns if the control plane doesn’t have the new endpoint yet?

Vivek Kulkarni (author): The command checks endpoint availability and returns a friendly error instructing to upgrade control plane first. The rest of the installer is unaffected; no behavior changes unless the command is invoked.

Kira Thompson (Tech Writing): Doc request: include a short section on how to find valid version IDs + an example of tenant-wide rollback.

Vivek Kulkarni (author): Added a section pointing operators to the control plane `/versions` endpoint and included a tenant-wide rollback example (model omitted).

source.title: deterministic-gpu-nodepool-stencil-tfmodule-checkpoint
source.author: Aisha Kapoor
source.release_notes: Add new terraform module 'gpu-nodepool-stencil' which enables deterministic GPU nodepool creation, lease-backed ephemeral IAM roles, and network-anchored tfstate checkpointing. Backwards-compatible migration path and a small migrator script included.
source.description: Context & motivation: Private deployments require predictable nodepool identities and safer rollback semantics when operators apply changes across air-gapped or peered VPCs. Existing modules create GPU nodepools with provider-generated names and rely on global tfstate only; this makes cross-account reconciliation and on-prem rollback brittle. This PR introduces a new terraform module (modules/gpu-nodepool-stencil) that provides deterministic naming, optional lease-backed ephemeral IAM bindings, and a lightweight tfstate checkpointing mechanism anchored to network tags. Goals: 1) deterministic, stable nodepool identifiers to allow safe rebinds and tenant moves; 2) lease-based IAM sessions so nodepools get least-privilege, short-lived credentials for cluster bootstrap; 3) checkpointed state objects (S3/GCS) tied to VPC/subnet tags to support orchestrated rollbacks and faster drift detection. What changed: - New module: terraform/modules/gpu-nodepool-stencil/ implementing deterministic naming strategy, nodepool fencing IAM templates, and optional network-anchor checkpointing hook. - New outputs and inputs to make module pluggable into existing private-installer stacks (vpc_id, subnet_tags, tenant_id, naming_seed). - Add tfstate-checkpoint script (scripts/tfstate_checkpoint.sh) used as a post-apply hook to write a minimal JSON checkpoint object to configured object store with network tag metadata. - Add migrator: scripts/tfstate_migrator_detname.go to compute deterministic names and optionally migrate state entries for existing installs (dry-run mode by default). - Add e2e-local test harness under test/integration/gpu-nodepool-stencil/ that runs a plan/apply against a local GCS-compatible backend (minio) to validate checkpoint creation and IAM binding lifecycle. - Documentation: docs/private-deploy/gpu-nodepool-stencil.md with examples and migration steps. - CI: terraform fmt/lint, unit tests for naming math, and an integration smoke test. Backwards compatibility & migration: The migrator is intentionally opt-in. The module has a toggle 'adopt_existing_names' and 'migrator_mode' with 'dry-run' by default; operators must run the migrator and confirm checkpoints before flipping adoption flags. Checklist: - [x] Terraform module implemented with full variable validation - [x] Post-apply checkpoint hook implemented and tested against minio - [x] Migrator included and documented - [x] Unit tests for naming algorithm - [x] Integration smoke test added - [x] Docs added and linked in private-installer README Testing & validation: Ran CI pipeline (format/lint/plan) — initially one formatting error, fixed in commit 3. Integration harness executed end-to-end on local minio and created checkpoint objects with expected metadata. Security & IAM: The module creates IAM role templates only; actual role assumption is gated by a 'lease' provider that issues ephemeral tokens (TTL configurable). The default behavior is conservative (no IAM creation unless 'enable_ephemeral_iam' is true). Migration notes: Run scripts/tfstate_migrator_detname.go --dry-run to preview changes, then create checkpoints for current state, then flip 'adopt_existing_names'. Related work: ENG-6342 (deterministic node ids), ENG-6401 (rollback checkpoints).
source.commits_summary: commit 1: scaffold gpu-nodepool-stencil module and variables; commit 2: add deterministic naming algorithm and unit tests; commit 3: add tfstate checkpoint hook and minio test fixtures; commit 4: add migrator tool and docs; commit 5: fix fmt, address lint failures and reviewer feedback; commit 6: add e2e smoke test and CI matrix updates.
review_comments: Liam Chen: Please ensure the naming seed is stable across upgrades; if we change seed format later we'll need a migration path.
Aisha Kapoor: The module exposes 'naming_seed' (string) and 'naming_version' (int). We added unit tests for versioned name derivation and included migrator to map old->new names.
Sofia Martinez: We need to avoid automatic IAM creation for orgs that manage roles centrally. Can you gate ephemeral role creation behind a flag?
Aisha Kapoor: Added 'enable_ephemeral_iam' defaulted to false and documented centralized-iam integration pattern.
Ethan Park: CI plan showed a terraform fmt failure and a flaky integration test on the first run.
Aisha Kapoor: Fixed fmt issue and increased integration test timeout; re-ran CI. All checks passed.
Sofia Martinez: Approved after confirming migrator is opt-in and docs include rollback steps.
Liam Chen: Approved. Recommend squash merge to keep history concise.
source.notes: This PR is intended to be low-risk by default. The default toggles avoid changing IAM or names unless operator opts in. The migrator and checkpoint objects provide a safe path for teams that want deterministic names for cross-account reconciliation.
source.egress_instructions: Operators: read docs/private-deploy/gpu-nodepool-stencil.md, run migrator with --dry-run, provision a checkpoint bucket and set backend config, then flip 'adopt_existing_names' only after validating checkpoints.
source.risk_assessment: Medium: introduces new state artifacts (checkpoint objects) and optional IAM templates. Risk is mitigated by opt-in flags, dry-run migrator, and unit/integration tests.
source.changelog_entry: Added GPU nodepool 'stencil' terraform module supporting deterministic naming, lease-backed ephemeral IAM templates, and tfstate checkpointing for safer rollback and rebind operations. Includes migrator and docs.
source.notes_for_release_team: Include note in private-installer release notes about the new module and emphasize the opt-in migration path. Recommend contacting platform engineering for org-wide IAM policies before enabling ephemeral IAM.
source.proof_of_concept_outputs: Sample checkpoint object created in minio under prefix 'checkpoints/vpc-12345/gpu-nodepool-stencil/2026-01-14T15:23:01Z.json' with metadata {"vpc_tag":"internal-prod","tenant":"tenant-42","module_version":"v1"} (redacted in repo).
