source.title: Add signing trust store + basic key rollover support for offline bundles
source.author: Ethan Park
source.description: Context: Redwood Private offline bundles will be cryptographically signed and must be verifiable in air-gapped installs. We need a trust model that supports (a) multiple trusted signing keys (during rotation) and (b) a predictable way for customers to update trust roots in offline environments without rebuilding the installer. This PR introduces a local trust store mechanism and implements basic key rollover support in the bundle verification path. Summary of changes: (1) Adds a trust store file format (JSON) that can contain multiple trusted public keys, each with a stable key_id (kid), algorithm, and optional metadata (created_at, not_before, not_after, comment). (2) Installer can load trust store keys from: default embedded trust store (compiled into the binary) OR an operator-provided trust store path (air-gapped friendly) OR an OS-level path (/etc/redwood-private/trust/signing-truststore.json) if present. Precedence: explicit CLI flag > env var > OS path > embedded. (3) Signature verification now selects the verification key by kid when present in the signature envelope; if kid is absent (migration case), we attempt verification against all trusted keys and require exactly one match (fail closed if 0 or >1 match). (4) Key rollover behavior: trust store supports multiple active keys simultaneously. Installer logs which kid was used, and emits a warning if the matched key is marked as "deprecated" in the trust store metadata (non-blocking; intended to help customers notice rotation lag). (5) Adds clear failure modes and remediation hints: unknown kid, empty trust store, malformed key material, and "multiple keys verify" ambiguity (points to duplicated keys / incorrect trust store content). (6) Adds unit tests covering trust store parsing, precedence rules, kid-based selection, and multi-key verification behavior. Notes / non-goals: This PR does not implement key revocation distribution, certificate chains, or customer-managed custom roots (future work tracked separately). It also intentionally does not add network fetches for trust material; offline installs must not depend on outbound connectivity. Follow-ups: docs PR to describe how to update the trust store in air-gapped environments and how rotation will be communicated; integration tests for tampered trust store file will land with the broader verification suite. Risk: low/medium. Changes are gated behind the offline bundle verification code path and default behavior remains "embedded trust store only" unless an operator configures overrides. Tested: unit tests; manual verification with two generated ed25519 keys and a signed manifest; confirmed fail-closed on unknown kid and on empty trust store.
commits: installer/verify: introduce TrustStore loader + precedence rules
signing: add key_id (kid) selection and multi-key verify fallback
config: add --trust-store and REDWOOD_TRUST_STORE_PATH
tests: trust store parsing and rollover scenarios
docs(dev): update internal README for trust store usage
review_conversation:
Marcus Lin: Can we guarantee we never accept an unpinned key when kid is missing? I want to avoid a scenario where multiple keys verify and we pick the first one.
Ethan Park: Yes. When kid is absent, we try all keys and require exactly one to verify; otherwise we fail with SIGNATURE_AMBIGUOUS and include remediation guidance.

Hanae Suzuki: Where does the embedded trust store come from and how do we update it for a hotfix? Also, can customers provide their own trust store without rebuilding the binary?
Ethan Park: Embedded trust store is a checked-in JSON blob updated as part of the release train. Customers can drop a trust store file into /etc/redwood-private/trust or point the installer at a path via --trust-store; no binary rebuild required.

Connor O'Brien: Please add a note on precedence order and ensure we log the resolved trust store path (but not key material).
Ethan Park: Added an info log: trust_store_source=(flag|env|os|embedded) and trust_store_path when applicable; key bytes never logged.

Owen Phillips: For support, can we surface the kid used in the logs? This will help with 'unknown key after rotation' tickets.
Ethan Park: Added kid to the verification summary line and included it in the preflight report output (without printing full public key).

Marcus Lin (request changes): Add a guard to fail if trust store contains duplicate kids.
Ethan Park: Implemented duplicate kid detection during load; now fails with TRUST_STORE_DUPLICATE_KID and lists the kid values.
release_notes: Installer now supports a local signing trust store with multiple trusted public keys (enables signing key rotation for offline bundles).

Operators can provide a trust store path via --trust-store or REDWOOD_TRUST_STORE_PATH; otherwise installer uses /etc/redwood-private/trust/signing-truststore.json if present, falling back to embedded keys.
Verification fails closed on unknown signing key ids (kid) and on ambiguous multi-key verification.

source.title: Add `bundle inspect` command for offline bundle diagnostics
source.author: Devon Price
commits: cli: introduce `bundle inspect` command scaffold + help text
bundle: implement manifest loading + schema validation + summary rendering
bundle: add signature verification wiring (trust store) + key ID display
bundle: add artifact hashing (sha256) with streaming + size/time guards
tests: unit tests for inspect output + golden fixtures for failure modes
docs: add examples for Support/SE workflows
source.description:
Problem
Support/SE and on-prem operators currently have to run a full preflight (or hand-parse the bundle) to answer basic questions like: “What version is this bundle?”, “Is the manifest present?”, “Which signing key signed it?”, and “Did the offline transfer corrupt anything?”. For air-gapped environments, we need an offline-first diagnostic command that can be run on a laptop/jump host without starting an install.

What this PR does
Adds a new CLI subcommand: `redwood-private-installer bundle inspect <bundle-path>`.
The command is designed to be safe to run on a customer machine and produces a concise report that can be pasted into a Support ticket.

Capabilities
- Locate and load the bundle manifest (current: `bundle.manifest.json`; supports the existing legacy name `manifest.json` for pilot bundles).
- Validate manifest schema (required fields present; version fields parse; artifact entries have name/path/hash).
- Print versions/build identifiers (bundle version, build ID, chart appVersions, runtime/model artifact versions when present).
- Verify signature when present (reads signature sidecar `bundle.manifest.sig` when available; otherwise reports “unsigned” with guidance).
- Verify trust chain using the installer trust store by default; optionally allow `--trust-store <path>` for air-gapped key rollouts.
- Compute and compare sha256 for each artifact entry (streaming) to catch corruption after transfer.

CLI usage
- `redwood-private-installer bundle inspect ./redwood-private-bundle-2026.01.0.tgz`
- `redwood-private-installer bundle inspect ./bundle/ --json`
- `redwood-private-installer bundle inspect ./bundle/ --no-hash` (fast path for very large bundles)
- `redwood-private-installer bundle inspect ./bundle/ --trust-store ./trust-store.pem`

Example output (text)
Bundle: ./redwood-private-bundle-2026.01.0.tgz
Manifest: OK (schema v1)
Bundle version: 2026.01.0  Build ID: priv-2026.01.0+build.4121
Signer: key_id=RWDSIGN-2025Q4-01  status=TRUSTED  signature=VALID
Artifacts: 37 verified (sha256)
Charts: redwood-core=2026.01.0 redwood-observability=2026.01.0
Notes: No network access required. For tamper/corruption failures, re-transfer bundle and re-run with `--json` for Support.

Output format
- Default: human-readable, stable labels intended for copy/paste into tickets.
- `--json`: machine-readable output (intended for future “support bundle” integration), includes error_code and failure_reason when verification fails.

Failure modes / error taxonomy
The inspect command intentionally mirrors the preflight error taxonomy where possible.
- Missing manifest: exits non-zero with `BUNDLE_MANIFEST_MISSING`
- Manifest parse/validation error: `BUNDLE_MANIFEST_INVALID`
- Signature missing: exit code remains 0 (informational) unless `--require-signature` is set
- Signature invalid/untrusted: non-zero with `BUNDLE_SIGNATURE_INVALID` or `BUNDLE_SIGNING_KEY_UNTRUSTED`
- Hash mismatch: non-zero with `BUNDLE_ARTIFACT_HASH_MISMATCH` and the offending path(s)

Security / privacy considerations
- No file contents are printed; only paths, sizes (optional), hashes, and version metadata.
- JSON output redacts absolute paths by default when the bundle is a directory (uses relative paths from bundle root).
- Does not attempt any outbound network calls (explicitly enforced by using only local filesystem readers).

Tests
- Golden tests for: valid signed bundle, unsigned bundle, missing manifest, invalid JSON, hash mismatch.
- Regression test for Windows path separators and spaces (pilot environments).

Docs
Adds a short CLI reference section under installer docs with troubleshooting examples for Support/SE.

Rollout
This is additive and safe. Support can start recommending `bundle inspect` immediately for air-gapped cases; preflight behavior is unchanged.

Related
- ENG-4826 (support tooling)
- PR #24844 (preflight signature verification)
- PR #24831 (manifest introduced)

Screenshots/logs
Included in comments below: sample output for both text and `--json`.

Checklist
- [x] Help text + examples
- [x] Unit tests + fixtures
- [x] Lint/format
- [x] Docs snippet
changed_areas: internal/bundle/hash_verify.go (streaming sha256; guardrails for large files)
internal/signing/verify.go (reuse verification entrypoint; expose key_id and trust status)
review_conversation: Ethan Park (review): The default behavior hashes all artifacts. For the larger pilot bundles this could be slow. Can we add a fast path / guardrails so Support doesn’t accidentally spend 20 minutes hashing a 200GB image tar?

Devon Price (author): Good call. Added `--no-hash` and also added a progress line every N files. Default remains hashing because it’s the most common failure after offline transfer, but I capped per-file read buffer and made hashing streaming.

Sean Gallagher (review): For on-call, please ensure error codes are stable and map cleanly to the preflight taxonomy. Also, print where the trust store was loaded from (embedded vs flag) so we can spot rotation issues.
Devon Price (author): Updated JSON output to include `trust_store_source` and `trust_store_key_ids`. Text output now includes a short “Trust store: embedded (2 keys)” line.
Marcus Lin (review): When signature is missing, should we exit non-zero? From a security posture perspective, I’d rather have this command make it obvious that “unsigned is not OK” going forward.

Devon Price (author): Added `--require-signature` to force non-zero on missing signature. Default remains informational to avoid breaking current pilot workflows and to keep inspect usable for debugging legacy bundles. We can flip default once migration is complete.
Dev Patel (review): Please add a copy/paste-friendly single-line summary for Support, plus a `--json` example in docs.
Devon Price (author): Done. Added “Summary:” line and expanded docs with both modes + troubleshooting guidance.
Ethan Park (approval): Looks good now. Thanks for the guardrails + `--require-signature` option.
Marcus Lin (approval): Approved with the condition that we track flipping defaults post-migration (filed follow-up in ENG).
Dev Patel (approval): Approved. This will materially reduce back-and-forth on SUP-31892-style cases.
source.merge_outcome: Merged via squash merge by Devon Price into main
release_notes: Private installer CLI: add `bundle inspect` for offline bundle diagnostics (manifest, signature, versions, artifact hash verification).

title: Add `bundle inspect` command for offline bundle diagnostics
preview:
Problem
Support/SE and on-prem operators currently have to run a full preflight (or hand-parse the bundle) to answer basic questions like: “What version is this bundle?”, “Is the manifest present?”, “Which signing key signed it?”, and “Did the offline transfer corrupt anything?”. For air-gapped environments, we need an offline-first diagnostic command that can be run on a laptop/jump host without starting an install.

What this PR does
Adds a new CLI subcommand: `redwood-private-installer bundle inspect <bundle-path>`.
The command is designed to be safe to run on a customer machine and produces a concise report that can be pasted into a Support ticket.

Capabilities
- Locate and load the bundle manifest (current: `bundle.manifest.json`; supports the existing legacy name `manifest.json` for pilot bundles).
- Validate manifest schema (required fields present; version fields parse; artifact entries have name/path/hash).
- Print versions/build identifiers (bundle version, build ID, chart appVersions, runtime/model artifact versions when present).
- Verify signature when present (reads signature sidecar `bundle.manifest.sig` when available; otherwise reports “unsigned” with guidance).
- Verify trust chain using the installer trust store by default; optionally allow `--trust-store <path>` for air-gapped key rollouts.
- Compute and compare sha256 for each artifact entry (streaming) to catch corruption after transfer.

CLI usage
- `redwood-private-installer bundle inspect ./redwood-private-bundle-2026.01.0.tgz`
- `redwood-private-installer bundle inspect ./bundle/ --json`
- `redwood-private-installer bundle inspect ./bundle/ --no-hash` (fast path for very large bundles)
- `redwood-private-installer bundle inspect ./bundle/ --trust-store ./trust-store.pem`

Example output (text)
Bundle: ./redwood-private-bundle-2026.01.0.tgz
Manifest: OK (schema v1)
Bundle version: 2026.01.0  Build ID: priv-2026.01.0+build.4121
Signer: key_id=RWDSIGN-2025Q4-01  status=TRUSTED  signature=VALID
Artifacts: 37 verified (sha256)
Charts: redwood-core=2026.01.0 redwood-observability=2026.01.0
Notes: No network access required. For tamper/corruption failures, re-transfer bundle and re-run with `--json` for Support.

Output format
- Default: human-readable, stable labels intended for copy/paste into tickets.
- `--json`: machine-readable output (intended for future “support bundle” integration), includes error_code and failure_reason when verification fails.

Failure modes / error taxonomy
The inspect command intentionally mirrors the preflight error taxonomy where possible.
- Missing manifest: exits non-zero with `BUNDLE_MANIFEST_MISSING`
- Manifest parse/validation error: `BUNDLE_MANIFEST_INVALID`
- Signature missing: exit code remains 0 (informational) unless `--require-signature` is set
- Signature invalid/untrusted: non-zero with `BUNDLE_SIGNATURE_INVALID` or `BUNDLE_SIGNING_KEY_UNTRUSTED`
- Hash mismatch: non-zero with `BUNDLE_ARTIFACT_HASH_MISMATCH` and the offending path(s)

Security / privacy considerations
- No file contents are printed; only paths, sizes (optional), hashes, and version metadata.
- JSON output redacts absolute paths by default when the bundle is a directory (uses relative paths from bundle root).
- Does not attempt any outbound network calls (explicitly enforced by using only local filesystem readers).

Tests
- Golden tests for: valid signed bundle, unsigned bundle, missing manifest, invalid JSON, hash mismatch.
- Regression test for Windows path separators and spaces (pilot environments).

Docs
Adds a short CLI reference section under installer docs with troubleshooting examples for Support/SE.

Rollout
This is additive and safe. Support can start recommending `bundle inspect` immediately for air-gapped cases; preflight behavior is unchanged.

Related
- ENG-4826 (support tooling)
- PR #24844 (preflight signature verification)
- PR #24831 (manifest introduced)

Screenshots/logs
Included in comments below: sample output for both text and `--json`.

Checklist
- [x] Help text + examples
- [x] Unit tests + fixtures
- [x] Lint/format
- [x] Docs snippet

source.title: automated-az-switchover-playbooks-terraform-helm
source.author: Ava Chen
source.description: Motivation: automate multi-AZ switchover for Private deployments and encode DR runbooks as code so runbooks are executable, testable, and versioned. This PR introduces: 1) a Terraform module that wires up cross-AZ routing and health-check failover for Private clusters in customer VPCs; 2) Helm chart hooks and readiness probes to ensure stateful services (controller, kv-store) drain cleanly before leader evacuation; 3) Ansible/Playbook-driven 'switchover' runbooks (playbooks-as-code) for operators to execute or run during scheduled drills; 4) a safe rollback utility and CI e2e drill job to validate the full failover and restore path. Scope: supports AWS multi-AZ (subnet-awareness), mechanisms for on-prem inventory mapping (rack/az tags), and a new 'drill-runner' CI job that will run synthetic traffic against canary replicas and validate KV sync and stateful reattach. Implementation notes: - terraform/modules/az-switchover: new module exposing variables for route53/elb-weighting, cross-AZ peering, and optional traffic-shift policies. - helm/redwood-private: added postStart/preStop hooks, new liveness/readiness templates, and values to control drain windows. - installer/playbooks/az_switchover.yml: idempotent playbook that performs detection, cordon, drain, shift routes, and verification steps; includes a --dry-run mode. - tools/drill-validate.sh: end-to-end validation runner used by CI and SRE during scheduled drills. - docs/drill-playbooks.md: operational runbook and decision tree generated from the playbooks (runbooks-as-code concept). Checklist: [x] Terraform module (initial implementation) [x] Helm hooks + templates [x] Playbook + dry-run support [x] CI e2e drill job added [x] Documentation and runbook generated [x] Tests: unit + e2e drills (observability validated) Backout plan: built into playbook; explicit rollback path documented and automated via terraform destroy/replicate previous routing weights to 100%. See 'backout_plan' field for high-level steps. Migration note: existing private clusters without AZ tags will continue to function; module is opt-in via installer flag. Related: references ENG-1723 and SRE-88 for on-call runbook alignment.
commits_summary: feat(terraform): add az-switchover module with routing and weight-shift API
feat(helm): add lifecycle hooks and drains for controller and kv-store
feat(playbook): add az_switchover.yml with dry-run and verification phases
chore(ci): add e2e-drill job and drill validator script
docs: add drill-playbooks.md and example runbook output
test(e2e): add synthetic traffic scenario and verification assertions
review_comments: Miguel Ortega: Can the module support Azure in a follow-up? If not, add explicit AWS-only docs.
Ava Chen: Opened a follow-up task ENG-1821 to add Azure support; AWS-only now and documented.
Priya Nandakumar: Please confirm the rollback window and add a no-op dry-run flag for on-call drills.
Ava Chen: dry-run implemented and CI exercise covers the no-op path; updated docs accordingly.
Ethan Brooks: Helm hooks look good but add unit tests for lifecycle template rendering.
Ava Chen: added template unit test; CI green after rerun.
source.drill_results: Executed staged drill against a non-production Private cluster in us-east-1 with three AZs. Observability checks: token-level latency increased by 5%-max during transition window, KV sync lag stayed under 200ms, no data loss observed. Drill took ~12 minutes end-to-end. Issues: initial timing assumption caused probe timeout on first run; adjusted probe timeouts and re-ran successfully.
source.backout_plan: High-level backout: 1) run tools/rollback/terraform-rollback.sh with the prior state artifact; 2) restore routing weights to previous values via aws cli helper (scripts/rollback/restore_weights.sh); 3) uncordon nodes via ansible playbook; 4) rollback helm chart to previous release using helm rollback. Playbook supports --force-rollback which reverses the verification steps. All steps are reversible and covered by CI drills.
source.release_notes: Adds opt-in automation for multi-AZ switchover and DR drills for Private deployments. Includes terraform module, helm lifecycle improvements, playbooks-as-code for operator runbooks, and an automated CI drill job. This enables scheduled, testable failovers and safe rollbacks.
notes_on_testing: Local test: helm template + unit tests for lifecycle hooks
CI: terraform validate + plan in a sandbox account
Drill: full e2e run against non-prod VPC with synthetic traffic
Post-merge: scheduled SRE drill with runbook owner in a month

source.title: Add canonical offline bundle manifest (hashes + artifact inventory) and wire into bundle build
source.author: Jordan Lee
source.description:
Context: Air-gapped / offline installs currently rely on “whatever is in the tarball” plus best-effort checksums produced by ad-hoc scripts. For offline signing + verification (follow-up PRs), the installer needs a deterministic, canonical manifest that enumerates bundle contents and pins hashes/versions. This PR introduces a v0 manifest file at the root of each offline bundle, updates the bundle generation tooling to produce it deterministically, and adds unit/integration tests to prevent regressions.

What’s included:
- New file in bundle root: redwood-bundle-manifest.json (canonical JSON; stable key ordering and artifact list ordering)
- Manifest schema v0.1:
  - schema_version, created_at_utc, bundle_id (build id), redwood_private_version, installer_min_version
  - hash_algorithm (sha256)
  - artifacts[] inventory with: path, kind (image-tar|helm-chart|binary|config|model-artifact|sbom|other), sha256, size_bytes, logical_name, version (optional), source (optional)
  - top-level bundle_sha256_over_artifact_hashes (sha256 over canonical concatenation of artifact entries; used for quick consistency checks)
- Deterministic build behavior:
  - Sort artifacts by path (byte-order) and normalize path separators to “/” in the manifest
  - Stream hashing for large artifacts to avoid loading into memory
  - Fail build if any artifact is missing or duplicate paths are detected
- Installer/tooling updates:
  - bundle build command now always emits the manifest and prints its path
  - “--offline” mode now asserts manifest presence (but does not yet enforce signature verification; that lands in PR 24844)
- Tests:
  - Golden manifest test for a small fixture bundle
  - Regression test ensuring stable output across runs (no non-deterministic ordering)
  - Tamper test: modifying a file changes its sha256 and causes manifest verification helper to fail

Compatibility:
- This PR does not change install semantics for existing unsigned bundles *yet*. The installer warns when a bundle is missing the manifest (to unblock incremental rollout), but bundle generation in our pipeline will start producing manifests immediately after merge.

Notes / follow-ups:
- Signing and verification will be layered on top of this manifest (detached signature over canonical manifest bytes).
- SBOM pointer + hash will be added once the SBOM artifact location is finalized (tracked separately).

Screenshots / examples:
- Example root layout:
  - /redwood-bundle-manifest.json
  - /images/*.tar
  - /charts/*.tgz
  - /manifests/*.yaml
  - /bin/redwood-private-installer

- Example manifest excerpt:
  - artifacts[0].path: "images/redwood-runtime_2025.11.0.tar"
  - artifacts[0].sha256: "<sha256>"
  - artifacts[0].kind: "image-tar"
commits: bundle: introduce manifest writer + schema types
bundle: stream sha256 hashing and normalize/sort artifact paths
installer: add manifest presence check + helper for reading inventory
tests: add golden fixture + determinism regression coverage
docs: update offline bundle generation notes (internal README)
changed_files: cmd/rpi/bundle_build.go (emit manifest, enforce deterministic ordering, stream hashing)
pkg/bundle/manifest/verify.go (verify helper for fixtures; used by tests and later by preflight)
cmd/rpi/install_offline.go (warn when manifest missing; plumb manifest path for later verification)
source.conversation:
Connor O'Brien (Release Eng) — 2025-11-21: We need to be extremely strict about determinism here. How are we canonicalizing JSON? If someone regenerates the same bundle on a different machine, do we get byte-for-byte identical manifest output?

Jordan Lee (author) — 2025-11-21: Added canonical writer that outputs UTF-8 JSON with sorted keys + stable indentation. Artifact list sorted by normalized path. Also added determinism_test that runs manifest generation twice with randomized filesystem walk order and asserts identical bytes.

Sahana Iyer (Build/CI) — 2025-11-22: Hashing reads the entire file into memory right now (io.ReadAll). That will blow up for image tars in real bundles.

Jordan Lee — 2025-11-22: Good catch. Switched to streaming hash via io.Copy into sha256.New(); added test that uses a 256MB sparse file fixture to ensure memory stays flat.

Marcus Lin (Security Eng) — 2025-11-23: Please document what exactly is covered by bundle_sha256_over_artifact_hashes. Also ensure path normalization is explicit (no \ on Windows). This becomes the root for signature verification later.

Jordan Lee — 2025-11-23: Updated docs and comments: bundle_sha256_over_artifact_hashes is computed over canonical lines "<sha256> <size_bytes> <path>" joined with "\n" (final newline included). Paths normalized to "/" and must be relative (no leading "/"; no ".." segments). Validation enforces that.

Ethan Park (Installer lead) — 2025-11-24: I’m OK with warning-only for missing manifest *for now*, but we should make it very obvious in logs that verification is not possible without it. Also can we make the warning message stable (so Support can search)?

Jordan Lee — 2025-11-24: Updated message to include code-like prefix: "OFFLINE_BUNDLE_MANIFEST_MISSING" and a remediation hint. This will become an error when we flip the default in the verification PR.

CI — 2025-11-25: integration tests failed (manifest golden mismatch)

Jordan Lee — 2025-11-25: Fixed non-deterministic timestamp field: created_at_utc now uses SOURCE_DATE_EPOCH if set; otherwise uses time.Now().UTC() but golden tests set SOURCE_DATE_EPOCH.

Dylan Brooks — 2025-11-26: Can we avoid embedding absolute build paths anywhere? I saw a local tmp path in a debug log.

Jordan Lee — 2025-11-26: Removed tmp path from log line; only log relative bundle paths.

Approvals:
- Ethan Park — Approved (2025-11-27)
- Connor O'Brien — Approved (2025-11-27)
- Marcus Lin — Approved (2025-11-27)
- Sahana Iyer — Approved (2025-11-27)
source.merge_outcome: Merged via squash; commit message: "Add canonical offline bundle manifest (hashes + artifact inventory)". Post-merge note: Release Eng will enable manifest generation in the Private offline bundle pipeline once PR 24844 lands (signature verification preflight).
source.release_notes: Internal: Offline bundle builder now emits redwood-bundle-manifest.json (schema v0.1) with sha256 hashes and artifact inventory; installer logs a stable warning if manifest is missing (verification enforced in follow-up).

text:
Context: Air-gapped / offline installs currently rely on “whatever is in the tarball” plus best-effort checksums produced by ad-hoc scripts. For offline signing + verification (follow-up PRs), the installer needs a deterministic, canonical manifest that enumerates bundle contents and pins hashes/versions. This PR introduces a v0 manifest file at the root of each offline bundle, updates the bundle generation tooling to produce it deterministically, and adds unit/integration tests to prevent regressions.

What’s included:
- New file in bundle root: redwood-bundle-manifest.json (canonical JSON; stable key ordering and artifact list ordering)
- Manifest schema v0.1:
  - schema_version, created_at_utc, bundle_id (build id), redwood_private_version, installer_min_version
  - hash_algorithm (sha256)
  - artifacts[] inventory with: path, kind (image-tar|helm-chart|binary|config|model-artifact|sbom|other), sha256, size_bytes, logical_name, version (optional), source (optional)
  - top-level bundle_sha256_over_artifact_hashes (sha256 over canonical concatenation of artifact entries; used for quick consistency checks)
- Deterministic build behavior:
  - Sort artifacts by path (byte-order) and normalize path separators to “/” in the manifest
  - Stream hashing for large artifacts to avoid loading into memory
  - Fail build if any artifact is missing or duplicate paths are detected
- Installer/tooling updates:
  - bundle build command now always emits the manifest and prints its path
  - “--offline” mode now asserts manifest presence (but does not yet enforce signature verification; that lands in PR 24844)
- Tests:
  - Golden manifest test for a small fixture bundle
  - Regression test ensuring stable output across runs (no non-deterministic ordering)
  - Tamper test: modifying a file changes its sha256 and causes manifest verification helper to fail

Compatibility:
- This PR does not change install semantics for existing unsigned bundles *yet*. The installer warns when a bundle is missing the manifest (to unblock incremental rollout), but bundle generation in our pipeline will start producing manifests immediately after merge.

Notes / follow-ups:
- Signing and verification will be layered on top of this manifest (detached signature over canonical manifest bytes).
- SBOM pointer + hash will be added once the SBOM artifact location is finalized (tracked separately).

Screenshots / examples:
- Example root layout:
  - /redwood-bundle-manifest.json
  - /images/*.tar
  - /charts/*.tgz
  - /manifests/*.yaml
  - /bin/redwood-private-installer

- Example manifest excerpt:
  - artifacts[0].path: "images/redwood-runtime_2025.11.0.tar"
  - artifacts[0].sha256: "<sha256>"
  - artifacts[0].kind: "image-tar"
